elements.sent-2411-003: Both methods have been widely applied, but little attention has been devoted to determining their merits and quantifying their uncertainties.

elements.sent-2411-005: Finally, it identifies the cause of velocity discrepancies for events measured using both methods.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additional sources of error were identified from the assumptions required by each method.
elements.sent-2411-007: To identify the source of velocity discrepancies, the velocities of two cases were measured and directly compared.
elements.sent-2411-008: These errors are relatively small compared to typical errors of 3000 km/s on this method.

elements.sent-2411-010: The spline fitting method tends to underestimate uncertainties by around 1000 km/s.
elements.sent-2411-011: This method can also be impacted by fine tuning of the smoothing parameters ($\sim$500-1000 km/s), but careful selection of these parameters can mitigate this.
elements.sent-2411-012: Optimum smoothing parameters for different cases are presented along with suggestions for best practice.

elements.sent-2411-015: This situation may be relatively rare among Ic-BLs, based on typical temperature evolution.

elements.sent-2411-017: Further research into the conditions under which spline fitting may produce larger velocities than the template fitting method is required, including comparisons of the results obtained with both methods to simulations.
elements.sent-2411-018: The morphology of the velocity evolution of an SN appears to be the same regardless of the method used.. \nolinenumbers.
elements.sent-2411-019: The optical spectra of supernovae tell us about their composition, the composition of their environment and their geometry.
elements.sent-2411-020: By measuring the observed wavelengths of prominent lines or features in these spectra, it is possible to infer the velocity of certain elements in the ejecta.
elements.sent-2411-021: The spectra of broad-line Ic supernovae (Ic-BLs) lack hydrogen and helium and are dominated by broad features of Fe, Si and Ca \citep[see e.g.][]{Ho.2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Their broad lines are the result of high expansion velocities, which are significantly larger than those of Ic supernovae \citep[e.g.][]{Modjaz.2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Ic-BL supernovae are of particular interest as they are the only supernova type linked with gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) \citep[e.g][]{Cano.201723 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: It has been proposed that the influence of the GRB jet may lead to differences in velocity or velocity evolution between Ic-BLs with and without GRBs (e.g. \citet{Finneran.2024B ; \citet{Modjaz.2016 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The velocities of Ic-BL features are most commonly measured using either the template fitting method \citep[e.g.][]{Srinivasaragavan.2024, Taddia.2019, Modjaz.2016 or by spline fitting methods \citep[e.g.][]{Finneran.2024B,DElia.2015, Schulze.2014,Patat.2001q8t .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: The template fitting method uses a Ic template spectrum which is broadened and blue-shifted until it matches the input Ic-BL spectrum \citep{Modjaz.2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Methods such as spline fitting interpolate the shape of the feature in question to determine the wavelength of the minimum flux, applying the doppler formula to determine the velocity \citep[e.g.][]{Silverman.2012, Patat.2001q8t .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Although both methods are widely used to measure the velocities of Ic-BLs, they are often implemented without a discussion of their uncertainties or their compatibility.
elements.sent-2411-029: The rapid expansion of the supernova ejecta means that the features of Ic-BL spectra are heavily blended.
elements.sent-2411-030: In regions where a feature is composed of multiple lines, it can be difficult to ascribe the minimum flux of a large feature to one line; for example the Fe II feature near 5000 \AA.
elements.sent-2411-031: This is likely to impact the velocities and velocity evolution produced by spline fitting methods, as shown by \cite{Prentice.2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The template fitting method was developed in order to handle exactly this scenario \citep{Modjaz.2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, it tends to produce larger errors than spline fitting, and often produces visually poor fits.
elements.sent-2411-034: The question remains whether these errors represent the true uncertainty on the velocity, or are over-estimates.
elements.sent-2411-035: Although this method relies on several assumptions, the impact of these, in particular their potential contribution to the overall error, has not yet been studied in detail.
elements.sent-2411-036: Although it may produce inaccurate results in regions with heavy blending, the spline fitting method offers some advantages.
elements.sent-2411-037: The errors produced by this method are generally smaller than those of template fitting, and the fits are generally of higher quality.
elements.sent-2411-038: However, smaller errors may be an indiction of under-estimation, which could produce false trends in velocity evolution.
elements.sent-2411-039: Another advantage is that the spline fitting method makes no assumptions about the underlying spectrum, and can be applied to all features in the Ic-BL spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-040: In contrast, the template fitting method cannot be applied to the silicon or calcium features of Ic-BLs.
elements.sent-2411-041: This is due to the differences in the spectra of Ic and Ic-BL supernovae in this region \citep{Modjaz.2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: This makes the spline fitting method much more useful for the analysis of multiple features for a large population of Ic-BLs \citep[e.g.][]{Finneran.2024B .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Previous studies have looked for evidence of differences in velocity between Ic-BLs with and without GRBs.
elements.sent-2411-044: At present these studies are hampered by small sample sizes, but it is expected that in the coming years the number of Ic-BLs, particularly those with a GRB detection, will grow.
elements.sent-2411-045: As a result it will be important to determine the optimum strategy for measuring the velocities of these events.
elements.sent-2411-046: Specifically, for some SNe, the spline-fitting method seems to produce velocities that are larger than those obtained by the template-fitting method, however this is not the case in all instances.
elements.sent-2411-047: This discrepancy raises the issue of which method should be trusted for Ic-BL supernova velocities.
elements.sent-2411-048: Indeed, this discussion is also relevant to supernova velocity determination more generally, as methods similar to the spline fitting method are often applied to other supernova subtypes \citep[e.g.][]{Silverman.2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Given the widespread usage of these two techniques, this paper focusses on a review of potential sources of error that are not currently accounted for in either method.
elements.sent-2411-050: In Sect. \ref{sec:smoothingerrors the impact of smoothing the spectra in both methods is determined, and some heuristics for determining the optimum smoothing parameters are developed.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Additional sources of error are identified and quantified for the template fitting method in Sect. \ref{sec:templatefitting , and for the spline fitting method in Sect. \ref{sec:splinesystematics .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: In Sect. \ref{sec:directcompare a direct comparison of both techniques for two test supernovae is used to identify potential factors leading to the velocity discrepancies found by previous studies.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Section \ref{sec:discussion suggests possible use cases for both methods, as well as mitigation techniques to reduce the impact of these novel sources of error.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Finally the conclusions are presented in Sect. \ref{sec:conclusion .}
elements.section-2411-002: Impact of spectrum smoothing on velocities
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.49\linewidth]{chapter5/1998bw_smoothing2.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.49\linewidth]{chapter5/2013dx_smoothing2.pdf} \caption[Effects of smoothing on the template fitting method 1]{Influence of the smoothing parameter, $k$, on velocity measured by the template fitting method for GRB980425-SN1998bw (left panel) and GRB130702A-SN2013dx (right panel). A smaller value of $k$ implies more severe smoothing.} \label{fig:keffects1}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.49\linewidth]{chapter5/1998bw_smoothing1.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.49\linewidth]{chapter5/2013dx_smoothing1.pdf} \caption[Effects of smoothing on the template fitting method 2]{Similar to Fig. \ref{fig:keffects1}, with the velocities scaled by the velocity for $k$=100. GRB980425-SN1998bw (left panel) and GRB130702A-SN2013dx (right panel)} \label{fig:keffects2}
elements.sent-2411-055: To reliably quantify the velocity of a given feature in a spectrum, some sort of smoothing algorithm must normally be applied to the data.
elements.sent-2411-056: The goal of all smoothing algorithms is to remove as much noise as possible from the raw data without introducing significant new features into the spectrum, or modifying existing features substantially.
elements.sent-2411-057: Spectral noise can complicate the process of determining the edges and minimum wavelengths of spectral features; for instance by creating local maxima or minima that do not truly represent the overall feature.
elements.sent-2411-058: Smoothing processes can leave visible differences in the processed spectrum, such as reduced peak heights and broadened features.
elements.sent-2411-059: This is particularly true for algorithms such as the Savitzky-Golay filter (SG, \cite{SG.1964 ), which preserves the peak area but not the height.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The template-fitting method, developed by \cite{Modjaz.2016 , uses the Fourier smoothing method described in \cite{Liu.2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: We proceed to describe the effects of smoothing on the velocities inferred from these two methods.
elements.sent-2411-062: A Fourier smoothing method similar to that used by \cite{Liu.2016 was developed for this analysis\footnote{An analysis was carried out to confirm that the results of the implementation used here match those of the method presented in the literature.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: This code is freely available at \url{https://github.com/GabrielF98/fouriersmooth .}
elements.sent-2411-064: This is the first publicly available Python version of the original IDL code \citep{Liu.2016 that implements this smoothing algorithm for supernova spectra.. .}
elements.sent-2411-065: The parameter $k$ can be varied to control the smoothing, with larger $k$ values implying weaker smoothing.
elements.sent-2411-066: This smoothing method was applied to GRB980425-SN1998bw and GRB130702-SN2013dx for this study; with three spectra for each GRB-SN: one pre-peak, one at peak and one post-peak.
elements.sent-2411-067: Their rest-frame phases were computed based on maximum light times given in \cite{Galama.1998 and \cite{DElia.2015 respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The redshifts and spectra are sourced from the GRBSN webtool\footnote{\url{grbsn.watchertelescope.ie \citep{Finneran.2024A and WISeREP\footnote{\url{https://www.wiserep.org/ \citep{WISEREP .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Each spectrum was smoothed using a range of $k$ parameters, before their iron velocities were estimated using the template-fitting method.
elements.sent-2411-070: In this analysis the following $k$ values were used: 25, 50, 100, 150, 200 and 300.
elements.sent-2411-071: These were chosen to include the values used in the literature, and to sample across a wider range.
elements.sent-2411-072: Figures \ref{fig:keffects1 and \ref{fig:keffects2 show the impact of changing the smoothing parameter ($k$) on the Fe II velocity measured by this method.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: As can be seen, velocities measured by the template-fitting method generally do not change significantly when the value of $k$ is changed.
elements.sent-2411-074: Perhaps one exception to this is $k$=25 which seems to have a large impact for both SNe at late times.
elements.sent-2411-075: This could be because $k$=25 represents a velocity of 12000 km/s, which is close to the iron velocity at late times in these supernovae \citep[e.g.][]{Finneran.2024B .}
elements.sent-2411-076: This could result in real features being removed by smoothing at this level.
elements.sent-2411-077: In other cases, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:keffects2 , the scatter introduced by changing $k$ contributes $\sim$500-1000 km/s to the uncertainty in velocity.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The uncertainties produced by the template fitting method will dominate over this effect.
elements.sent-2411-079: This appears to be the case both before, at and after the SN peak time.
elements.sent-2411-080: In conclusion, the choice of $k$ is not overly relevant in determining the velocity of a supernova using this method, although its contribution to the final errors cannot be fully neglected.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Smoothing in the spline fitting method

elements.sent-2411-081: There are two tuneable smoothing parameters in the spline fitting method: the number of knots used for the spline, known as ``spline density''; and the width of the SG filter used to smooth the spectrum, called ``filter width''.
elements.sent-2411-082: We aim to find heuristics for choosing the optimum filter width and spline density.
elements.sent-2411-083: In real applications these parameters should be determined on a case-by-case basis, using the original spectrum as a reference point.
elements.sent-2411-084: This section also looks at the typical errors introduced by the smoothing of the input spectrum.

elements.section-2411-003: Additional sources of error in template fitting
elements.sent-2411-128: The age of a spectrum or photometric datapoint is defined relative to a reference epoch.
elements.sent-2411-129: Supernova reference epochs are typically determined from the lightcurve\footnote{Though it is possible to estimate the age from the spectrum, for example \cite{Mazzali.2000 .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-130: As there are several epochs to choose from, astronomers must balance the ease of determining the date of a particular epoch against its accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-131: One of the most common supernova epochs is the date of maximum light, also called the peak time.
elements.sent-2411-132: Peak times can be determined using an analytical formula such as the Bazin model \citep[e.g.][]{Taddia.2015, Bazin.2011 , by quadratic fitting \citep[e.g.][]{Bianco.2014 , or by interpolation.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: The benefit of using peak time is that it is relatively straightforward to measure, since photometry near the peak is normally well-sampled with low scatter.
elements.sent-2411-134: However, the epoch of maximum light is energy dependent; with the date differing between observing filters.
elements.sent-2411-135: This is shown in Table \ref{tab:peaktimes and is illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:maxepoch , where the peak time for several filters has been determined using spline interpolation for SN2020bvc.}}

elements.sent-2411-240: There have been several attempts to quantify the expansion velocities of Ic-BL supernovae over the years.
elements.sent-2411-241: Many of these attempts rely on just two methods: the spline fitting \citep[e.g.][]{Finneran.2024B or the template fitting method \citep{Modjaz.2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-242: Whilst both methods had been widely applied, little attention had been devoted to which method is the most performant.
elements.sent-2411-243: This analysis has sought to answer that question by quantifying the additional sources of uncertainty present in both methods, and performing direct comparisons using test cases.
elements.sent-2411-244: A number of additional sources of error were identified by this analysis, and the validity of claims made about these methods have been tested.
elements.sent-2411-245: In this section an interpretation of the results is provided, and recommendations for best practice are offered.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Lessons from direct comparison of the methods
elements.sent-2411-246: The analysis presented in Sect. \ref{sec:directcompare compared the velocities produced by both methods for two GRB-SNe.}
elements.sent-2411-247: This investigation confirmed that the template fitting method is capable of handling cases of severe blending, as was suggested by \cite{Modjaz.2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-248: However, it was noted that significant errors are commonplace prior to de-blending.
elements.sent-2411-249: These errors are in the range of 2000-5000 km/s depending on the event and epoch.
elements.sent-2411-250: It was believed that spline fitting would fail for both test-events, as they both show similar levels of blending of the iron triplet.
elements.sent-2411-251: In the case of GRB980425-SN1998bw this belief was borne out.
elements.sent-2411-252: However, for GRB130702A-SN2013dx, the spline fitting method seems to be unaffected by blending.
elements.sent-2411-253: This suggests that different blending conditions exist which impact both methods differently.
elements.sent-2411-254: A proposal was put forward which explains the impact of blending with regard to the slope of the underlying continuum.
elements.sent-2411-255: Assuming continua which are dominated by thermal effects, it is possible to gauge how often blending may be an issue for spline fitting.
elements.sent-2411-256: Their results suggest that temperatures decline to a plateau of 5000-6000 K within the first 20 days after the explosion.
elements.sent-2411-257: This suggests that, at least during this period, many Ic-BLs will have iron features on blue continua.
elements.sent-2411-258: For this reason, it is likely reasonable to apply the spline fitting method for many Ic-BLs.
elements.sent-2411-259: This also means that the velocities measured in \cite{Finneran.2024B are not affected by issues with blending in all cases.}
elements.sent-2411-260: Furthermore, it was discovered that the shape of the velocity evolution for GRB980425-SN1998bw was identical for the two methods, despite issues arising from the blending and the underlying continuum.
elements.sent-2411-261: This is because the region which forms the iron feature forms each line in the iron triplet, and so they all undergo the same evolution.
elements.sent-2411-262: This means that velocity evolution studies for the iron feature are likely to produce very similar results, provided that one line is followed throughout the evolution.
elements.sent-2411-263: If different lines are followed once de-blending occurs, there will be discontinuities in the velocity evolution.
elements.sent-2411-264: In terms of absolute velocities, in cases where the continuum is biased against the spline fitting method, there may be differences in velocity of up to 15000 km/s between the two methods.
elements.sent-2411-265: Cumulatively these events would increase the average velocity for a population of Ic-BLs.
elements.sent-2411-266: Since the optical properties of Ic-BLs with and without GRBs are similar, this probably does not impact comparisons between these populations.
elements.sent-2411-267: Resolution of blending issues alone likely is not enough to separate the two populations.
elements.sent-2411-268: However, when comparing these populations to Ic supernovae, spline fitting might lead to a larger difference than exists in reality (assuming that the true values are similar to those seen with the template fitting method).
elements.sent-2411-269: The magnitude of this effect is dependent on the number of Ic-BLs where the continuum will cause this problem.
elements.sent-2411-270: Further work is needed to determine how often this occurs.
elements.sent-2411-271: This work should include modelling of the SED or spectra of a large population of Ic-BLs with and without GRBs.
elements.sent-2411-272: Given the issues posed by blending, it is tempting to stick with the template fitting method.
elements.sent-2411-273: However, as pointed out by \cite{Modjaz.2016 , this method struggles to fit the silicon feature of Ic-BL supernovae.}
elements.sent-2411-274: If performing an analysis of multiple features, it is advantageous to use the same method on all features, as the magnitude and sources of error will be consistent for each group of features.
elements.sent-2411-275: In this case, mitigation strategies are needed to reduce the impacts of blending and the continuum.
elements.sent-2411-276: One approach may be to remove the blackbody contribution from the spectrum prior to fitting.
elements.sent-2411-277: Since the continuum in the blue to UV region is likely not purely blackbody, so this approach may meet with challenges.
elements.sent-2411-278: Another tactic would be to fit the iron feature `in reverse', by starting with the latest non-nebular spectra, and identifying the 5169 \AA\,line, before following it backward in time until it disappears.
elements.sent-2411-279: This would likely produce the correct evolution and velocity for this line, since it tends to de-blend prior to the other two lines in the triplet.
elements.sent-2411-280: The disadvantage of course is that this will reduce the number of useable spectra for velocity measurements, because the line will likely be blended at early times.

elements.sent-2411-281: The remainder of this analysis was devoted to determining what the additional sources of error in each method might contribute to the overall uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-282: In Sect. \ref{sec:smoothingerrors the impacts of smoothing algorithms on the final result was determined for each method.}
elements.sent-2411-283: In the case of the spline-fitting method, the fine-tuning of smoothing parameters was found to have a larger impact for low resolution or low S/N spectra; in these cases, errors may approach 3000-4000 km/s.
elements.sent-2411-284: In higher resolution/higher S/N cases, the typical error due to variation of the smoothing parameters is of order 1000 km/s.
elements.sent-2411-285: In most cases these errors are comparable to the one sigma errors produced by the method.
elements.sent-2411-286: For the template fitting method, smoothing may constitute an additional uncertainty of around 500-1000 km/s.
elements.sent-2411-287: In many cases the effect of this is marginal, because the errors produced by template fitting tend to be between 2000-5000 km/s at early times.
elements.sent-2411-288: However, at late times this source of error may become more relevant.
elements.sent-2411-289: In general however, smoothing algorithms do not significantly affect the result for a population, except in cases of extreme parameter choice which can be avoided through sensible visual examination of the spectra.
elements.sent-2411-290: This is probably because the presence of broad features in the Ic-BL spectrum provides more leeway with smoothing parameters, since these broad features are unlikely to be lost when smoothed.
elements.sent-2411-291: The presence of a phase-shifts when using the the template fitting method, reviewed in Sect. \ref{sec:phase , may shift the velocity by 500-2000 km/s (or more in some cases).}
elements.sent-2411-292: These types of errors are likely to be very common in practice, since the template fitting method requires you to know the phase of each spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-293: The phase cannot always be determined with arbitrary accuracy, either because V-band data are not available or because a broad peak exists.
elements.sent-2411-294: Although it would be possible to use the explosion epoch for GRB-SNe, this tactic is probably not generally applicable for Ic-BLs, since finding the explosion epoch can often be harder than finding the peak.
elements.sent-2411-295: If the spectral templates used in this method were available every day rather than every 2 days, it would go some way to solving this issue.
elements.sent-2411-296: In theory this would be achievable with the existing Ic spectra used to make these templates.
elements.sent-2411-297: Phase shifts cannot be ignored at late times, since their contribution becomes similar to that of the typical error on the template fitting method.
elements.sent-2411-298: The impacts of phase shift will be present across the whole spectral evolution, as it arises when the peak time is not determined very precisely.
elements.sent-2411-299: This could mean that the shape of the evolution will be impacted, by either making the decay faster and then shallower or slower and then steeper.
elements.sent-2411-300: It is important to note that this effect is not detectable without prior knowledge of the velocity.
elements.sent-2411-301: This is one reason why verifying the results of any of these methods against simulations should be a priority in future.
elements.sent-2411-302: The spline fitting method tends to under-estimate the error on velocity.
elements.sent-2411-303: This typically contributes an extra 1000 km/s to the velocity, as determined from the residuals for the power-law and broken power-law fits.
elements.sent-2411-304: It is not entirely clear what the source of these errors is.
elements.sent-2411-305: They may be a result of slight differences in spectral resolution or alignment between different spectra.
elements.sent-2411-306: It cannot necessarily be avoided when using a heterogeneous dataset, so quantifying it is essential.
elements.sent-2411-307: However, even including this error, the spline fitting method is more precise than the template fitting method.
elements.sent-2411-308: Accuracy of the method was verified by comparing it to the template fitting method for SN2013dx, where the effects of blending are minimal.
elements.sent-2411-309: Given the benefits of the applicability of the spline fitting method to all the features in the Ic-BL spectrum, it remains a useful tool for measuring the velocities of Ic-BLs.
elements.sent-2411-310: The large errors produced by the template fitting method are due to a wide range of factors, which were not discussed here.
elements.sent-2411-311: The construction of Ic template spectra almost certainly has an impact on the velocity errors.
elements.sent-2411-312: Data presented in \cite{Modjaz.2016 shows that Ic supernovae reach a velocity plateau of around 8000 km/s, 20 days after V-band maximum light.}
elements.sent-2411-313: Prior to this, their velocity declines from around 13000 km/s.
elements.sent-2411-314: Given that spectra are averaged in 5 day windows, there may be significant velocity differences in the pre 20 days window.
elements.sent-2411-315: Furthermore it was observed during the use of the template fitting method that poor fits are sometimes produced, as measured by the $\chi_r^{2}$ statistic or by visual examination.
elements.sent-2411-316: Both of these effects could make it difficult for the MCMC analysis to converge to a precise solution.
elements.sent-2411-317: However, the exact mechanism behind the large errors produced by this method are beyond the scope of this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-318: It is simply something worth bearing in mind for cases where spline fitting might be applicable.
elements.sent-2411-319: This paper has reviewed the two major methods used to measure the velocity of Ic-BL supernovae.
elements.sent-2411-320: These are often applied without regard to their sources of error or true benefits.
elements.sent-2411-321: The focus of this work was to quantify and develop strategies to deal with these errors, and to identify whether either method performs better in all cases.
elements.sent-2411-322: This was accomplished through the use of test cases and exploration of the parameter spaces of each method.
elements.sent-2411-323: It was found that both methods are susceptible to uncertainty associated with smoothing, but that this source of error tends to be small relative to the overall error.
elements.sent-2411-324: Accounting for its impact may be more important for the spline fitting method because it has smaller errors than template fitting.
elements.sent-2411-325: In light of this, there is no motivation to improve smoothing algorithms or to fine tune smoothing parameters beyond a simple sanity check.
elements.sent-2411-326: In the case of the template fitting method, the potential for phase discrepancies creates an additional source of error, which may contribute an additional $\sim$1000 km/s to the final error when added in quadrature.
elements.sent-2411-327: The size of this error seems to increase the further the given spectrum is from peak light; at peak, phase effects are probably negligible.
elements.sent-2411-328: Phase effects arise mainly because of the limited choice of template spectra, which forces the rounding of phase.
elements.sent-2411-329: However, in some cases the phase may be impacted by an inaccurate supernova peak time, as this method uses the V-band maximum for its epoch.
elements.sent-2411-330: One proposed solution for this is to use the GRB trigger time epoch where possible.
elements.sent-2411-331: However, this method cannot be applied to non GRB-associated Ic-BLs.
elements.sent-2411-332: In these cases the peak time is no worse than the explosion time, but it is easier to determine.
elements.sent-2411-333: For the spline fitting method, the largest additional source of uncertainty appears to be due to random scatter of the measured velocities.
elements.sent-2411-334: This could perhaps be caused by alignment issues between spectra, or instrumental differences.
elements.sent-2411-335: This typically contributes around 1000 km/s of additional error.
elements.sent-2411-336: This is significant in the context of this method, as it tends to produce smaller errors than template fitting.
elements.sent-2411-337: A direct comparison of both methods was used to verify the claim that the template fitting method can handle blended features.
elements.sent-2411-338: In instances where a red continuum is present in the region of the iron feature, the template fitting method performs reasonably well, but the spline fitting method over-estimates the velocity.
elements.sent-2411-339: In instances where a blue continuum is present both methods can be used.
elements.sent-2411-340: This means that, assuming that the template fitting velocities are correct, the spline fitting method over-estimates the velocity for a certain percentage of Ic-BLs.
elements.sent-2411-341: However, it may be possible to identify such cases by simple examination of the spectra.
elements.sent-2411-342: Two possible ways to reduce the impact of blending on the spline fitting method could be: flatting the spectra in a similar way to the template fitting method; or, in the case of a large sample, to apply the spline fitting method but trace features from the late-time spectra rather than beginning with early features.
elements.sent-2411-343: Overall the template fitting method seems to the most optimal method to use when dealing with cases of severe blending.
elements.sent-2411-344: In summary, there does not seem to be a clear optimum method to measure the velocity of a Ic-BL supernova.
elements.sent-2411-345: Although the spline fitting method produces more precise estimates of velocity in some instances, it fails to handle blending in all cases, resulting in higher velocity measurements relative to template fitting.
elements.sent-2411-346: This may be due to the underlying blackbody temperature of the spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-347: The template fitting method can handle these cases, but with greater uncertainty in velocity, which will affect its ability to constrain the velocity evolution of SNe, especially in the case of large population studies.
elements.sent-2411-348: In any case, both methods seem to produce identical velocity evolution in all instances.

elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{chapter5/2016P_silverman_analysis_Fe.pdf} \caption[Variation of Fe II velocity with filter width and spline density for a sample spectrum of SN2016P]{Similar to Fig. \ref{fig:knotsmoothSi1998bw}, but instead showing the Fe II velocities of SN2016P; which is an example of a low-resolution, very low S/N spectrum. It appears that the low S/N may have washed out the impact of smoothing on the velocity, the range of all distributions is large, and the typical one-sigma error is 3000-5000 km/s. The effect of the spline density is also altered. Using the case of 2 knots makes little difference here, and there is no preferred spline density. The choice of smoothing parameters may shift the mean velocity by $\sim$2000-3000 km/s in such cases.} \label{fig:knotsmoothFe2016P}
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{chapter5/1998bw_silverman_analysis_Fe.pdf} \caption[Variation of Fe II velocity with filter width and spline density for a sample spectrum of GRB980425-SN1998bw]{Similar to Fig. \ref{fig:knotsmoothSi1998bw}, but instead showing the change in Fe II velocities of GRB980425-SN1998bw with filter width and spline density; in the case of a spectrum with a high resolution and high S/N. The results are broadly similar to Fig. \ref{fig:knotsmoothSi1998bw}. Here the variation in mean velocity due to fine tuning of smoothing parameters is $\sim$1000 km/s.} \label{fig:knotsmooth1998bwFe}
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{chapter5/2003jd_silverman_analysis_Fe.pdf} \caption[Variation of Fe II velocity with filter width and spline density for a sample spectrum of GRB030329-SN2003jd]{Similar to Fig. \ref{fig:knotsmoothSi1998bw}, but instead showing the change in Fe II velocities of GRB030329-SN2003jd with filter width and spline density; in the case of a spectrum that has a high-resolution and a reasonable S/N. The results are similar to those in Fig. \ref{fig:knotsmoothSi1998bw}. In this case the fine tuning of the smoothing parameters causes variation in the mean velocity of 1000-1500 km/s.} \label{fig:knotsmooth2003jdFe}
elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{chapter5/2003jd_silverman_analysis_Si.pdf} \caption[Variation of Si II velocity with filter width and spline density for a sample spectrum of GRB030329-SN2003jd]{Similar to Fig. \ref{fig:knotsmoothSi1998bw}, but instead showing the change in Si II velocities of GRB030329-SN2003jd with filter width and spline density; in the case of a spectrum that has a high-resolution and a reasonable S/N. Optimum filter width is 1.5-5\%, with no preference for knot density except that 2 knots should not be used. In this case the fine tuning of the smoothing parameters causes variation in the mean velocity of $<$1000 km/s.} \label{fig:knotsmooth2003jdSi}
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{chapter5/2020bvc_silverman_analysis_Fe.pdf} \caption[Variation of Fe II velocity with filter width and spline density for a sample spectrum of SN2020bvc]{Similar to Fig. \ref{fig:knotsmoothSi1998bw}, but instead showing the change in Fe II velocities of SN2020bvc with filter width and spline density; in the case of a spectrum that has a low-resolution and a high S/N. The lower resolution appears to have resulted in larger ranges for some of the violin plots, and typical one sigma errors are 3000-4000 km/s. In this instance, as for SN2016P it is difficult to choose optimum parameters, though it appears 5\% filter width and 5\% knots may be suitable. Varying the smoothing parameters in this instance may lead to mean velocity changes of order 3000 km/s.} \label{fig:knotsmooth2020bvcFe}
elements.figure-2411-022: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{chapter5/2020bvc_silverman_analysis_Si.pdf} \caption[Similar to Fig. \ref{fig:knotsmoothSi1998bw} showing similar results for the Si II feature of SN2020bvc]{Similar to Fig. \ref{fig:knotsmoothSi1998bw}, showing the change in Si II velocities for SN2020bvc with filter width and spline density; in the case of a spectrum that has a low-resolution and a high S/N. Optimum parameters appear to be 20\% knot density with any filter width. It is particularly clear that 10\% knots performs poorly in this instance. Variation of these parameters may contribute an error of $\sim$4000 km/s, on top of typical 1 sigma errors of $\sim$2500 km/s. This large error is likely a result of the low resolution, which also explains why a higher knot percentage is required.} \label{fig:knotsmooth2020bvcSi}
